content:
7 min read
Just now

--

A 19-year-old’s confession about debugging, doubt, and finally deciding to hit publish.
It’s 1:47 AM.
I just fixed a bug by adding a single comma.
Not a clever fix.
Not some breakthrough.
A comma.
I’d spent close to forty minutes convinced something was deeply, structurally wrong with my code. I reread the function five times, out loud at one point, like saying it slower would reveal the problem. I opened a few more tabs — which, statistically, never helps, but I do it anyway every single time, like opening the fridge again hoping something new appeared.
Then I saw it.
One comma.
Forty minutes.
I didn’t feel relieved.
I felt a little stupid, honestly, in the specific way where you can’t even be mad at anyone but yourself, so you just sit there in silence for a second, recalibrating.
I texted my roommate “found it” with no context. He didn’t ask. He’s used to this by now.
Instead of closing my laptop and pretending this never happened, like a reasonable person would, I opened a blank document and started typing about it.
That document became this.

For years, I thought blogs like this belonged to people who’d already arrived.
I had a picture in my head of who gets to write about technology.
Someone with years behind them.
Someone whose code worked the first time — or at least looked like it did from the outside, which I now realize is most of what “looking experienced” actually means.
Someone who never had to paste an error message into Google at midnight and quietly hope a stranger from 2016 had already solved it for them, in a thread with three replies, the last one saying “bump.”
I was not that person.
I once spent twenty-two minutes debugging a “missing module” error before realizing I’d been running the file from the wrong folder the entire time. I didn’t tell anyone that for a week. I’m telling you now, which feels like a strange kind of progress.
So I waited.
I told myself I’d start writing once I knew more. Once a project actually impressed someone who wasn’t my mom. Once I felt like I belonged in the conversation instead of quietly standing near the edge of it, nodding at words I’d have to look up later.
That feeling never really arrived on schedule.
I’m starting to think it doesn’t, for anyone. I think people just get tired of waiting for it and start anyway.

Then I noticed what I actually enjoyed reading.
The posts I kept coming back to weren’t always written by the most accomplished engineers I could find.
They were written by people who admitted things.
When something broke, they said so, specifically, including the embarrassing part — not “I encountered an issue” but “I deleted the wrong database and found out our backups hadn’t run in three weeks.”
When a decision turned out to be wrong, they explained why they’d made it anyway, under deadline, half-asleep, trusting a tutorial they hadn’t fully read.
A post about a failed side project taught me more than a polished success story, almost every time. Not because failure is interesting on its own — it usually isn’t, it’s mostly just tedious — but because it leaves a trail. You can follow someone’s actual thinking instead of the cleaned-up version they present after the fact.
Those are the posts I bookmark. I have a folder of them. It’s called, unimaginatively, “good ones.”
Somewhere along the way, I stopped thinking writing was something you did after reaching the destination, and started thinking it was more like leaving a trail of breadcrumbs for whoever’s a few steps behind you. Including, probably, me, in six months, having forgotten all of this.

My browser is, right now, a genuine mess.
I counted before writing this paragraph: thirty-one tabs.
A few are documentation pages I opened looking for one specific answer, scrolled through twice, and never found it, because it turns out the thing I needed was in a different version of the docs entirely.
A few are GitHub issues where someone describes my exact problem in painful, specific detail, and then the thread just stops. No resolution. The last comment is from 2021. Someone named xYzdev_throwawayXxYyZz says "any update on this?" and nobody ever answers them. I think about that person sometimes. I hope they figured it out.
There’s a YouTube video promising a two-minute fix that is, in practice, eleven minutes, four of which are the guy explaining why he started this channel.
There’s a Stack Overflow answer marked “Solved” where the actual fix is a single comment that says:
“nvm fixed it lol”
No further explanation. Just vibes.
Somewhere between the documentation tab I needed and the YouTube video I clearly didn’t, I realized I’d spent more time looking for a fix than I would’ve spent just rereading my own code from the top, slowly, like a person with patience.
I don’t have patience. That’s part of why I’m telling you this.

Some projects turn into products. Some just turn into me, slightly different than before.
I’ve built things that worked exactly the way I planned. Not many. A handful.
I’ve also built a lot of things that, generously, taught me something about myself instead.
There was one project — an automation tool I was sure would finally be the thing — where I had the architecture mapped out in a notebook before I’d written a single line of code. I’d already mentally drafted how I’d describe it later. That should have been my first warning sign. I’ve since learned that the more confident I feel before starting something, the worse week I’m probably about to have.
It didn’t catastrophically fail.
It did something slower and worse.
It almost worked.
Every fix uncovered a new problem hiding directly behind the old one, like one of those nesting dolls except every doll is also slightly on fire. Every late night ended with something a little closer to functional, and nowhere near actually functional.
At one point I genuinely considered just renaming the whole repo to abandoned and walking away with my dignity intact. I didn't. Mostly out of stubbornness, not virtue.
I don’t remember most of the technical details anymore.
I remember sitting on my floor at one in the morning, laptop open on my bed, seriously asking myself if this was the moment I quit.
I remember not quitting, mostly because quitting felt like more effort than continuing at that point.
It never became the thing I originally pictured. It became proof that I can sit with something difficult longer than I think I can, which turns out to be a more useful skill than whatever the original project was supposed to do.

There’s a specific, slightly unhinged joy in code finally agreeing to work.
The terminal stops complaining.
The output matches the thing that’s been living in my head for three days, half-formed, refusing to leave me alone even during unrelated conversations.
And somehow, I still don’t trust it.
I run it again.
Still works.
I run it a third time, slower, watching the screen like it’s about to confess something.
Only then do I let myself believe it, and even then, only about sixty percent.
I’ve laughed out loud, alone, at a terminal window, at 2 AM, for no audience, over something nobody else would understand without three paragraphs of context. I’m not even slightly embarrassed about that anymore. I used to be.
Nobody sees these moments. They’re not the kind of thing that goes on a resume. But they’re the actual reason I keep doing this, more than any of the reasons I’d give if you asked me in an interview.

Why write any of this down instead of letting it disappear?
I have hundreds of scattered notes by now.
Half-finished markdown files with names like notes2.md, which tells you exactly how organized I was that day.
A Notion page literally titled “fix later,” last edited four months ago, untouched since, monument to a problem I apparently decided to simply live with instead.
Code comments that say:
// don't touch this, I genuinely don't know why it works
— left as a warning to a future version of me who will, without question, touch it anyway, because I have never once respected a warning I wrote for myself.
Folders named:
final
final2
final2_actual
final2_actual_thisone
I’m aware this is a problem. I have not fixed it. Writing this paragraph is, in a weird way, the closest thing to therapy these folder names have ever gotten.
Somewhere inside all of that mess are lessons that took real hours to learn, and most of them would quietly evaporate if I didn’t put them somewhere on purpose. Not because they stopped mattering. Just because memory has a habit of sanding the hard parts smooth until you forget they were ever hard at all.
Writing slows that down.
It also exposes, embarrassingly often, the parts I didn’t actually understand as well as I thought I did while I was busy congratulating myself for solving them.
That part stings a little. It’s also, I think, the entire point of doing this publicly instead of in a private note no one will read.

So. Day one.
This isn’t a tutorial.
There’s no checklist at the bottom, no five-step framework, no promise that any of this changes your life by Tuesday.
It’s just an explanation for why I’m here, written by someone who, twenty minutes ago, was still deciding whether “crime scene” was too dramatic a comparison for a browser tab situation. (It’s not. I checked again. Still thirty-one.)
What comes after this will look different. Some of it technical. Some of it a full walkthrough of a project from the first bad idea to whatever it eventually limped into becoming. Some of it, probably, a far-too-detailed account of a bug that ruined a perfectly good Saturday.
I genuinely don’t know where this goes from here. Not in the calculated, “the uncertainty is the journey” sense people say in interviews. The actual, real sense, where I have no plan past this paragraph and I’m okay with that.
What I do know is that waiting to feel ready wasn’t getting me any closer to writing the first sentence. The only thing that ever got me closer was sitting down and writing a bad first draft, then a slightly less bad second one, then this.
So this is it.
Day one.
A blank page that isn’t blank anymore.
I’ll figure out the rest of it the same way I figure out everything else — badly, first, with way too many tabs open, and a comma I probably forgot somewhere in this very article.
